output_text:
{"covered_dims": {"R1.1": 0.6, "R1.2": 0.8, "R1.3": 0.9, "R1.4": 0.9, "R1.5": 0.4, "R1.6": 0.6, "R1.7": 0.85}}

Summary: critique with concrete revisions. The piece reads cleanly but oversells novelty, leaves a mechanical ambiguity at the core of the method, and asserts headline numbers without scaffolding the reader to trust them.

**1. Mechanical ambiguity in the gating signal.** The text says the meta-model uses "residual statistics over a 50-sample sliding window" to predict the PID law's expected tracking error. Residuals of *what*? If the PID is not actually running, you cannot have PID residuals; if PID and MPC both run, the compute savings story collapses. Three plausible designs all fit the prose:
- (a) MPC is the active controller and the predictor uses MPC's plant-model residuals as a proxy for nonlinearity.
- (b) PID runs in shadow on the model.
- (c) Features come from reference / setpoint changes plus measured outputs, not residuals at all.
Pick one and say it explicitly in the first paragraph of the method-motivating passage. As written, a reviewer cannot reason about whether the gating signal is causally available.

**2. "Per-sample, query-conditional" is contradicted by the 50-sample window.** A 50-sample feature window means the decision is at best a 50-sample-lagged response to regime changes — that is exactly the regime where a constraint violation would occur. Either reframe ("per-sample dispatch with a 50-sample feature window for regime estimation") or justify why the lag is acceptable.

**3. The "first" claims will not survive review.** Both novelty claims are too strong against existing literatures the piece does not engage with:
- Event-triggered and self-triggered MPC (Heemels, Lehmann et al.) selects between "solve QP" and "hold/cheap update" per sample.
- Explicit MPC (Bemporad et al.) and learned MPC surrogates (Hertneck, Hewing, Chen & Zeilinger; Karg & Lucia) trade solve cost for a lookup/inference under exactly this motivation.
- Supervisory / switched control (Morse, Liberzon) and gain scheduling have long performed regime-conditional controller selection.
- Cross-plant transfer of learned controllers / surrogates has been reported (Lale et al.; meta-RL for control).
Recommended rewrite: drop "first framework" and "first demonstration." Replace with a positioned delta, e.g., "Unlike event-triggered MPC, which gates on a Lyapunov-style trigger derived from the plant model, CostMPC gates on a learned predictor of *closed-loop tracking error*, allowing dispatch decisions conditioned on user-specified performance tolerance rather than model-derived bounds." That is a defensible contribution; "first" is not.

**4. The hedge "we believe constitutes the first..." weakens rather than protects.** It signals you have not searched the literature. Either cite the closest prior work and explain the delta, or remove.

**5. Headline numbers need scaffolding even at this length.** "62% fewer solver invocations, IAE within 3%" reads as a single point estimate. The reader needs at minimum: averaged over how many runs, what disturbance/noise regime, what threshold setting (the ablated knob from Section 5 directly affects this number, so it is not a single number). One additional clause — "averaged over N seeds across the standard TE disturbance suite at tolerance τ=…" — would make this much harder to dismiss.

**6. The PID-vs-MPC indistinguishability premise needs softening for TE.** Tennessee Eastman is a multivariable plant where a single PID law is widely understood not to track well; practitioners use decentralized PID *banks* with detuning. State which PID structure you compare against ("a tuned decentralized PI structure following Ricker's loop pairing" or similar). Otherwise the motivating premise reads as overstated.

**7. "Cheap PID law" — singular vs. bank.** Same point: for TE, the singular noun is a tell that the description has been smoothed for the abstract. Clarify.

**8. "QP solve cost on every sample" understates the alternatives.** A reader thinking about explicit MPC will note that the QP cost is not intrinsic — it is a property of online MPC. One sentence acknowledging this and explaining why CostMPC is preferred to explicit MPC (table-size blow-up at TE-scale state dimension, presumably) would close a real gap.

**9. Safety mention is too thin to leave to Section 6.** A supervisory selector that can route to a controller incapable of respecting constraints is a safety-critical artifact. Even one sentence in the framing — what guarantee (if any) holds: probabilistic bound, fallback-to-MPC on tolerance violation, runtime monitor? — would change how a reader weights the rest.

**10. Name.** "CostMPC" reads as a variant of MPC. It is a dispatcher. Consider a name that signals the supervisory role (e.g., "GateMPC," "SelMPC," "DispatchCtrl"). Minor, but reviewers latch onto this.

**11. Forward references "Section 5… Section 6…" in the closing paragraph.** Fine if this is an intro; jarring if it is an abstract. Confirm placement and drop if the latter.

**12. Sentence-level polish.**
- "expensive nonlinear MPC" → "nonlinear MPC (NMPC)" on first use; subsequent uses can drop "expensive" (the cost framing is already established).
- "sit well inside the linear regime" — informal; "lie within a near-linear operating regime" is more standard.
- "the latter is invoked uniformly, paying its quadratic-program solve cost on every sample" — passive and slightly contorted; "yet the QP is solved at every sample regardless" is tighter.
- "with only 4% IAE degradation after a brief calibration phase" — "brief" is unquantified; give samples or seconds.

**Proposed minimal rewrite of the novelty paragraph:**

> CostMPC trains a lightweight meta-model — a two-layer MLP over a 50-sample window of [SPECIFY: e.g., one-step model residuals and reference deltas] — to predict whether the PID law would, at the current operating point, achieve closed-loop tracking error within a user-specified tolerance τ. When the predicted error is below τ, the sample is dispatched to PID; otherwise the QP is solved. Unlike event-triggered MPC [cite], which gates on model-derived triggering conditions, CostMPC gates on a learned predictor of closed-loop *performance* against a user-set tolerance; unlike explicit MPC [cite] and learned MPC surrogates [cite], it preserves the online QP as the high-fidelity fallback rather than replacing it.

Uncertainty disclosure: the specific prior works cited above (Heemels/Lehmann on event-triggered MPC; Bemporad on explicit MPC; Hertneck / Chen & Zeilinger / Karg & Lucia on learned MPC surrogates; Morse / Liberzon on switched control) are named from recall and should be verified before citation. The substantive claim — that strong "first" assertions in this area face well-established prior literatures — is robust regardless of which specific references you settle on.
